args_redacted.query: zero trust architecture NIST 800-207 principles never trust always verify
defense in depth layered security strategy network application data

args_redacted.raw:
{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
COMPARISON: ZERO TRUST SECURITY vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
    A security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always verify."
    It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the traditional
    network perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or system should be
    trusted by default — even if already inside the network. Every access
    request must be authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
    A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
    controls to protect information assets. The idea is that if one layer
    fails, the next layer continues to provide protection. It originates from
    military strategy and applies controls across physical, technical, and
    administrative domains.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
    • Never Trust, Always Verify — Every access request is treated as
      potentially hostile, regardless of origin.
    • Least Privilege Access — Users and systems receive only the minimum
      permissions necessary to perform their function.
    • Micro-Segmentation — Networks are divided into small, isolated zones
      so that lateral movement by attackers is severely limited.
    • Continuous Monitoring & Validation — Sessions and behaviors are
      continuously inspected and re-verified; trust is never permanent.
    • Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised,
      designing controls to minimize blast radius and detect intrusions.
    • Identity-Centric — Identity (user, device, application) becomes the
      primary perimeter, replacing the traditional network perimeter.
    • Explicit Verification — Use multiple signals (identity, device health,
      location, data sensitivity) to make access decisions.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
    • Layered Protection — Deploy multiple, independent security controls
      at different layers (physical, network, host, application, data).
    • No Single Point of Failure — Redundancy ensures that the failure of
      one control does not result in complete compromise.
    • Diversity of Controls — Use different types of controls (preventive,
      detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors/technologies.
    • Perimeter-Based Foundation — Traditionally relies on a strong outer
      perimeter (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with additional internal layers.
    • Administrative Controls — Policies, procedures, training, and
      awareness programs form a critical layer.
    • Physical Security — Guards, locks, cameras, and access controls
      protect the physical infrastructure layer.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| ASPECT              | ZERO TRUST                     | DEFENSE IN DEPTH              |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Core Philosophy     | Trust nothing; verify          | Layer controls; expect        |
|                     | everything continuously.       | breaches at any layer.        |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Perimeter Model     | No implicit trust in network   | Strong outer perimeter with   |
|                     | location; identity is the      | internal layers; some trust   |
|                     | new perimeter.                 | inside the perimeter.         |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Access Control      | Granular, per-session,         | Often role-based or network-  |
|                     | dynamic, context-aware.        | based; may be more static.    |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Lateral Movement    | Actively prevented via micro-  | Slowed by layers but not      |
|                     | segmentation and continuous    | always prevented at the       |
|                     | verification.                  | identity/session level.       |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Trust Model         | Trust is never granted         | Trust is earned at the        |
|                     | implicitly; continuously       | perimeter and may persist     |
|                     | evaluated and short-lived.     | within the network.           |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Primary Focus       | Identity, data, and sessions.  | Infrastructure, network       |
|                     |                                | topology, and systems.        |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Historical Context  | Modern (formalized ~2010 by    | Traditional (military origin, |
|                     | Forrester; NIST SP 800-207).   | adopted in IT for decades).   |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|

================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST SECURITY
    A security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always verify."
    It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the traditional
    network perimeter, and therefore no user, device, or system should be
    trusted by default — even if already inside the network. Every access
    request must be authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH
    A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
    controls to protect information assets. The idea is that if one layer
    fails, the next layer continues to provide protection. It originates from
    military strategy and applies controls across physical, technical, and
    administrative domains.


2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
    • Never Trust, Always Verify — Every access request is treated as
      potentially hostile, regardless of origin.
    • Least Privilege Access — Users and systems receive only the minimum
      permissions necessary to perform their function.
    • Micro-Segmentation — Networks are divided into small, isolated zones
      so that lateral movement by attackers is severely limited.
    • Continuous Monitoring & Validation — Sessions and behaviors are
      continuously inspected and re-verified; trust is never permanent.
    • Assume Breach — Operate as if the network is already compromised,
      designing controls to minimize blast radius and detect intrusions.
    • Identity-Centric — Identity (user, device, application) becomes the
      primary perimeter, replacing the traditional network perimeter.
    • Explicit Verification — Use multiple signals (identity, device health,
      location, data sensitivity) to make access decisions.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
    • Layered Protection — Deploy multiple, independent security controls
      at different layers (physical, network, host, application, data).
    • No Single Point of Failure — Redundancy ensures that the failure of
      one control does not result in complete compromise.
    • Diversity of Controls — Use different types of controls (preventive,
      detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors/technologies.
    • Perimeter-Based Foundation — Traditionally relies on a strong outer
      perimeter (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with additional internal layers.
    • Administrative Controls — Policies, procedures, training, and
      awareness programs form a critical layer.
    • Physical Security — Guards, locks, cameras, and access controls
      protect the physical infrastructure layer.


3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| ASPECT              | ZERO TRUST                     | DEFENSE IN DEPTH              |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Core Philosophy     | Trust nothing; verify          | Layer controls; expect        |
|                     | everything continuously.       | breaches at any layer.        |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Perimeter Model     | No implicit trust in network   | Strong outer perimeter with   |
|                     | location; identity is the      | internal layers; some trust   |
|                     | new perimeter.                 | inside the perimeter.         |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Access Control      | Granular, per-session,         | Often role-based or network-  |
|                     | dynamic, context-aware.        | based; may be more static.    |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Lateral Movement    | Actively prevented via micro-  | Slowed by layers but not      |
|                     | segmentation and continuous    | always prevented at the       |
|                     | verification.                  | identity/session level.       |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Trust Model         | Trust is never granted         | Trust is earned at the        |
|                     | implicitly; continuously       | perimeter and may persist     |
|                     | evaluated and short-lived.     | within the network.           |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Primary Focus       | Identity, data, and sessions.  | Infrastructure, network       |
|                     |                                | topology, and systems.        |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Historical Context  | Modern (formalized ~2010 by    | Traditional (military origin, |
|                     | Forrester; NIST SP 800-207).   | adopted in IT for decades).   |
|---------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|


4. HOW THEY COMPLEMENT EACH OTHER
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Zero Trust and Defense in Depth are not mutually exclusive. In fact, they
work best when combined:

    • Zero Trust can be seen as a specific, modern implementation of the
      Defense in Depth philosophy — it layers identity,
